steps.title: Lay out the two given facts
steps.content: (1) Twelve-day-old nestlings beg more in response to their own local dialect than to nonlocal dialects — an early preference for local song. (2) Song preference plays a role in mate selection. The completion must be the logical consequence of combining these two.

steps.title: Chain them forward
steps.content: Early preference for local dialect + song preference shapes mate choice $\Rightarrow$ when these nestlings mature, that preference should push them toward mates from their own local population rather than nonlocal ones.

steps.title: Eliminate by overreach or contradiction
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'preference... likely disappears as they mature': contradicts the inference — the whole reason the early preference matters is that it carries forward into mate selection. ✗
  • Choice B — 'likely to receive more food from their caretakers': the study measured begging-call counts, not feeding outcomes; nothing in the passage links dialect preference to food rewards. Unsupported. ✗
  • Choice C — 'drives them when they mature to reproduce with other F. hypoleuca from local rather than nonlocal populations': directly follows the chain — early local-dialect preference plus song-driven mate choice yields local mating. ✓
  • Choice D — 'preference for both local F. hypoleuca dialects and the songs of other local songbirds': extends the finding to other species, which the study never tested. Overreach. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
